license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Windows Defender protection history deleted
entries.summary: A history item was removed from the Windows Defender protection history.
entries.details: Generated when an item is removed from Windows Defender's protection history. Protection history records past detections, quarantine actions, and remediation events. Deletion of history items — particularly around the timeframe of a compromise — can obscure the record of what was detected and what actions were taken. Correlate with EID 1116 and EID 1117 entries in the same timeframe to determine whether history deletion follows a detection event, which is a strong anti-forensic indicator.

investigationPivots: Correlate the deletion timestamp with nearby EID 1116/1117 events — deletion shortly after a detection is a strong cleanup indicator
Identify what process or user account triggered the history deletion via Security EID 4688/4624 correlation
Consider retrieving Defender history from alternate sources (MDE portal, SIEM) if local history was deleted
commonFalsePositives: Users or IT manually clearing Defender history via the Windows Security app
Automated cleanup policies removing old history entries

entries.title: Suspicious behavior detected
entries.summary: Windows Defender detected a potentially suspicious behavior.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender's behavioral monitoring detects a suspicious activity pattern. Unlike EID 1116 (signature-based detection), EID 1015 fires on behavioral heuristics — such as a process performing operations characteristic of credential dumping, code injection, or persistence installation — even if no known malware signature matches. Key fields: Process Name, the behavior type or category, and the affected resource. Behavioral detections that do not result in a subsequent EID 1116/1117 (no signature match) may represent novel or fileless threats.

investigationPivots: Process Name is the primary pivot — behavioral detections in LOLBAS tools (LOLBins: mshta.exe; LOLBAS Scripts: powershell.exe, wscript.exe) or Office applications are high priority
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to review the full process ancestry and command line
A 1015 with no subsequent 1117 may mean Defender flagged but did not remediate — the activity may still be active
commonFalsePositives: Security testing and red team tools triggering behavioral heuristics
Legitimate software performing unusual but benign operations (e.g., custom backup tools, scripting automation)

entries.title: Malware or unwanted software detected
entries.summary: Windows Defender detected a threat.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender detects malware or potentially unwanted software. Key fields: Threat Name, Category, Severity Level, Detection Source (real-time, on-access, scheduled scan, etc.), Process Name (the process associated with the detection), and Path (the file or object flagged). Correlate Threat Name against the detection timeline to identify what the attacker was running when Defender fired. Process Name is especially valuable — detections attributed to LOLBAS tools (e.g., powershell.exe and wscript.exe as LOLBAS Scripts; mshta.exe, certutil.exe, and rundll32.exe as LOLBins) are higher priority than detections in temp directories.

investigationPivots: Pivot on Process Name to identify what was executing the detected payload
Correlate Path with file creation events to determine how the file arrived on the system
Detection Source of 'Real-Time Protection' means the threat was caught during active execution; 'Scheduled Scan' may indicate an older dormant file
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 to find related process activity
commonFalsePositives: Security research tools or red team utilities flagged as PUA/PUP
Packed or obfuscated legitimate software triggering heuristic detections

detectionRules.title: Malware Detected by Windows Defender
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 1116
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Threat Name">' ThreatName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Process Name">' ProcessName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Severity Name">' Severity '</Data>' *
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ThreatName, ProcessName, Severity
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Adapt field parsing to your ingestion method. In MDE-integrated environments, use the DeviceAlertEvents or SecurityAlert table for richer context.

detectionRules.title: Windows Defender Malware Detection
detectionRules.rule:
title: Windows Defender Malware Detection
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 1116
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Security research tools
  - Packed legitimate software
level: high

entries.title: Antivirus took action to protect machine from malware
entries.summary: Windows Defender took a remediation action on a detected threat.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender performs a remediation action following a detection (EID 1116). Key fields: Threat Name, Action (Quarantine, Remove, Allow, Block, Clean), Action Status (success or failure), and ThreatID. Action Status of failure means the threat was detected but not successfully remediated — the file or process may still be active. Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to link detection and response. An action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' may indicate the threat was excluded or the user overrode the detection.

investigationPivots: Action Status failure means the file or process may still be present — escalate immediately
Action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' warrants investigation into whether an exclusion was in place
Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to get the full detection context including file path and process
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software quarantined due to false positive detection
Security tooling excluded from scanning triggering no-action outcomes

detectionRules.title: Defender Remediation Action Failed
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 1117
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Action Name">' ActionName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Action Status">' ActionStatus '</Data>' *
| where ActionStatus !has "Successfully"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ActionName, ActionStatus
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: A failed remediation means the threat may still be active. Escalate immediately and investigate with EID 1116 context.

detectionRules.title: Windows Defender Remediation Action
detectionRules.rule:
title: Windows Defender Remediation Action
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 1117
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate software quarantined due to false positive
level: medium

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in block mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule blocked a potentially malicious operation.
entries.details: Generated when an Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rule fires in block mode and prevents an operation. Key fields: the rule GUID (identifies which ASR rule triggered), Process Name (the process that attempted the blocked operation), and Path (the target file or object). Each ASR rule GUID maps to a specific rule such as 'Block Office applications from creating executable content' or 'Block credential stealing from Windows LSASS'. An ASR block during an incident is a high-confidence signal that an attacker technique was attempted and stopped.

investigationPivots: Map the rule GUID to a specific ASR rule using Microsoft's ASR rule reference to understand what technique was blocked

Process Name reveals what attempted the blocked operation — flag Office apps or LOLBAS tools (scripting engines such as wscript.exe, cscript.exe, powershell.exe; LOLBins such as mshta.exe, regsvr32.exe, rundll32.exe)
Correlate timestamp and process with Security EID 4688 to build the full execution chain
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Office macros or scripts blocked by overly broad ASR rules
Developer tools triggering code-execution ASR rules

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in audit mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule would have blocked an operation but is in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when an ASR rule fires in audit mode — the operation was allowed to proceed but would have been blocked in enforcement mode. Key fields are identical to EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, and Path. Audit mode events are valuable during incident investigations to identify attacker techniques that were attempted but not blocked, particularly in environments where ASR rules have not yet been moved to enforcement. Treat audit events from known-bad processes with the same urgency as block events.

prerequisites.description: Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules must be enabled and configured in audit mode via Microsoft Defender, Intune, or Group Policy.

investigationPivots: Audit mode means the operation succeeded — treat as executed attacker activity, not just an attempt
Same pivot fields as EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, Path
Use audit events to identify which ASR rules should be promoted to block mode
commonFalsePositives: Same as EID 1121 — audit mode means no blocking occurred, so false positive impact is limited to alert noise

entries.title: Security intelligence update succeeded
entries.summary: Windows Defender security intelligence (signatures) updated successfully.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender successfully updates its security intelligence (antimalware signatures). Key fields: the new signature version and the update source. Monitoring the cadence of EID 2001 events is useful for establishing a baseline — a host that suddenly stops receiving updates (no 2001 events for an extended period) may be isolated from update sources, have update mechanisms disabled, or be experiencing network-level blocking of update traffic consistent with a compromised host trying to avoid signature-based detection of its malware.

investigationPivots: Absence of EID 2001 events over an extended period may indicate update mechanisms have been disabled or blocked
Correlate last successful update timestamp with the timing of a compromise to determine whether signatures were outdated when the attack occurred
Hosts with significantly outdated signature versions are higher-risk targets
commonFalsePositives: Air-gapped or isolated hosts that rely on manual signature updates
Hosts on metered connections with update scheduling configured to minimize bandwidth

entries.title: Security intelligence update failed
entries.summary: Windows Defender failed to update its security intelligence.
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender security intelligence update fails. Key fields: error code and the update source that was attempted. Repeated EID 2002 failures on an otherwise internet-connected host may indicate that the host cannot reach Microsoft update servers — potentially because an attacker has modified the hosts file, DNS, firewall rules, or proxy settings to block updates and prevent new signatures from detecting their tools. Correlate with network log sources to determine whether update traffic is being blocked at the host or network level.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Persistent update failures on an internet-connected host may indicate blocked update traffic — investigate hosts file, DNS, and firewall rules
Correlate with EID 5007 to check whether proxy or update server configuration was recently changed
An update failure immediately preceding a malware execution event indicates the host may have been left intentionally unprotected
commonFalsePositives: Transient network connectivity issues causing temporary update failures
Proxy or firewall misconfiguration blocking Windows Update traffic

entries.title: Real-time protection is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender real-time protection was turned off.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender real-time protection is disabled. This is a high-priority alert during an incident — disabling real-time protection is a common attacker technique to prevent detection of subsequent payloads. Correlate the timestamp with process creation and logon events to identify what account or process disabled protection. Legitimate causes include Group Policy-managed AV exclusions, third-party AV coexistence, and IT administrative actions; these should be validated against change management records.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what process or account made the change
Check whether the change corresponds to a known Group Policy or administrative action
Real-time protection disabled immediately before a malware detection or payload execution is a strong compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators temporarily disabling protection for maintenance or software installation
Third-party AV products disabling Defender as part of coexistence management
Group Policy enforcing a disabled state on managed endpoints

detectionRules.title: Windows Defender Real-Time Protection Disabled
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 5001
| project TimeGenerated, Computer
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: Windows Defender Real-Time Protection Disabled
detectionRules.rule:
title: Windows Defender Real-Time Protection Disabled
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 5001
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - IT administrators temporarily disabling for maintenance
  - Third-party AV managing Defender coexistence
level: high

entries.title: Real-time protection configuration changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender real-time protection setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when a real-time protection-specific configuration setting is changed. While EID 5007 captures all configuration changes, EID 5004 specifically flags modifications to real-time protection settings such as monitoring scope, behavior monitoring, network protection, or on-access scanning. These changes are higher priority than generic configuration changes because they directly affect the ability to detect active threats. Key field: the specific real-time protection setting that was changed and its new value.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: The changed setting and its new value are the key pivot — changes reducing scan coverage or disabling behavioral monitoring are high priority
Correlate with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify the process and account that made the change
EID 5004 paired with EID 5001 in close succession confirms real-time protection was both reconfigured and disabled
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy or MDM applying real-time protection policy changes
Third-party AV management tools modifying Defender real-time settings for coexistence

entries.title: Antimalware platform configuration changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender configuration setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when any Windows Defender configuration setting changes. Key field: the old and new configuration values, recorded in the format 'HKLM\...\value = old value -> new value'. Configuration changes of particular interest include additions to ExclusionPath, ExclusionProcess, or ExclusionExtension (attackers add exclusions to prevent detection of their tools), and changes to DisableRealtimeMonitoring, DisableBehaviorMonitoring, or DisableIOAVProtection. Exclusion additions should be correlated against known software deployments.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Changes to ExclusionPath or ExclusionProcess are the highest-priority — they may be hiding attacker tooling
Correlate the changed value with the file or directory path to determine what is being excluded
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what made the configuration change
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installers adding Defender exclusions during setup
Group Policy or MDM pushing exclusion policies to managed endpoints

detectionRules.title: Defender Exclusion Added via Configuration Change
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 5007
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="New Value">' NewValue '</Data>' *
| where NewValue has_any ("ExclusionPath", "ExclusionProcess", "ExclusionExtension")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, NewValue
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Exclusion changes are the highest-priority 5007 variant. The NewValue field shows the registry path and new value in 'key = value' format.

detectionRules.title: Windows Defender Exclusion Added
detectionRules.rule:
title: Windows Defender Exclusion Added
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 5007
    NewValue|contains:
      - 'Exclusion'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate software adding exclusions during installation
  - Group Policy managing exclusion policies
level: high

entries.title: Antimalware engine failure
entries.summary: The Windows Defender antimalware engine encountered an error and failed.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Defender antimalware engine encounters a fatal error and fails. While engine failures can be caused by resource constraints or software bugs, they can also result from deliberate interference — an attacker crashing or corrupting the Defender engine to prevent detection of subsequent activity. Key field: the error code. Correlate the engine failure timestamp with nearby process creation events, network connections, or file writes to identify whether the failure coincides with attacker activity.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate the engine failure timestamp with Security EID 4688 to identify process activity occurring immediately before or after the failure
Engine failures that cannot be explained by known software issues or resource constraints warrant investigation into potential tampering
Check whether the engine failure was followed by a recovery (EID 5001 state change) or left the system unprotected
commonFalsePositives: Engine crashes due to bugs in specific Defender versions — check for pending updates
Resource exhaustion on underpowered systems causing engine failures

entries.title: Scanning for malware and unwanted software is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender malware and PUP scanning was disabled.
entries.details: Generated when scanning for malware and potentially unwanted programs (PUP) is disabled. This is distinct from EID 5001 (real-time protection disabled) — scanning can be selectively disabled while real-time protection remains nominally enabled. Disabling malware scanning creates a window during which new files can be written to disk and executed without signature-based detection. Treat this event with the same urgency as EID 5001 during an active incident.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what process/account made the change
Files written to disk after scanning was disabled should be treated as potentially unscanned and uninspected
Correlate with EID 5007 to identify the specific registry key or configuration value changed
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy-managed configurations that selectively disable certain scanning features
Temporary administrative disablement for performance testing or software compatibility troubleshooting

entries.title: Controlled Folder Access blocked an application
entries.summary: Controlled Folder Access prevented an application from modifying a protected folder.
entries.details: Generated when Controlled Folder Access (CFA) blocks an application from writing to a protected folder. CFA is designed to protect high-value locations (Documents, Desktop, Pictures, and custom paths) from unauthorized modification — primarily ransomware encrypting files. Key fields: the blocked application path and the folder it attempted to modify. An unknown or unsigned process attempting to modify protected folders is a high-confidence ransomware or data tampering indicator. Correlate with Security EID 4688 and Sysmon EID 1 to review the full ancestry of the blocked process. A CFA block event that is not explained by known software is a tier-1 incident response trigger.

prerequisites.description: Controlled Folder Access must be enabled in Microsoft Defender via Group Policy, Intune, PowerShell (Set-MpPreference -EnableControlledFolderAccess Enabled), or Windows Security settings.

investigationPivots: The blocked application path is the primary pivot — flag anything in temp directories, AppData, or user-writable paths
Correlate with Sysmon EID 1 to identify the full process tree that spawned the blocked application
A CFA block from a known-good application may indicate process injection — the legitimate process may have been hijacked to deliver the write
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate applications not on the CFA allowlist attempting to write to monitored folders (e.g., backup tools, syncing utilities)
IT-deployed software that was not added to the CFA allowed applications list

detectionRules.title: Controlled Folder Access Block
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 1123
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Process Name">' ProcessName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Path">' TargetPath '</Data>' *
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ProcessName, TargetPath
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Any occurrence from an unexpected process should be treated as a high-priority incident until ruled out.

detectionRules.title: Controlled Folder Access Block Event
detectionRules.rule:
title: Controlled Folder Access Block
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 1123
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate applications not added to CFA allowlist
level: high

entries.title: Controlled Folder Access audited an application write attempt
entries.summary: Controlled Folder Access detected a protected folder write attempt but allowed it (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Controlled Folder Access (CFA) is in audit mode and detects an application attempting to write to a protected folder. Unlike EID 1123 (block mode), the write was permitted to proceed — this event is a detection-without-prevention signal. Audit mode CFA events carry the same investigative weight as block events: an unknown process writing to protected folders in audit mode may be actively encrypting or tampering with data. Key fields are identical to EID 1123: blocked application path and target folder. Treat audit events from unknown or unsigned processes as incidents in progress, not hypothetical detections.

prerequisites.description: Controlled Folder Access must be enabled and configured in audit mode via Microsoft Defender, Intune, PowerShell, or Group Policy.

investigationPivots: Audit mode means the write succeeded — if the triggering process is malicious, data may already be modified or encrypted
Same pivots as EID 1123: application path, process ancestry, target folder
Use audit events to identify which applications need to be added to the CFA allowlist, and which should be promoted to block
commonFalsePositives: Same as EID 1123 — legitimate software writing to protected folders without being on the allowlist

entries.title: Network Protection audited a connection to a malicious URL or IP
entries.summary: Network Protection detected a connection to a malicious or untrusted destination (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Microsoft Defender Network Protection detects a process attempting to connect to a URL or IP address that is classified as malicious, phishing, or untrusted, and is in audit mode. The connection was permitted to proceed. Key fields: the process name initiating the connection and the destination URL or IP. Audit mode Network Protection events are detection-without-prevention and indicate that the host reached or attempted to reach attacker-controlled infrastructure. Correlate the destination with threat intelligence. Common triggering scenarios include: browser-based phishing page loads, malware C2 beacon attempts, and drive-by download connections. Network Protection operates at the kernel level (HTTP.sys) and covers both browser and non-browser processes.

prerequisites.description: Microsoft Defender Network Protection must be enabled and set to audit mode via Group Policy, Intune, or PowerShell (Set-MpPreference -EnableNetworkProtection AuditMode). Requires Microsoft Defender Antivirus with real-time protection enabled.

investigationPivots: The destination URL or IP is the primary pivot — correlate against threat intelligence for C2 infrastructure, phishing domains, or known malware distribution hosts
Process Name identifies what initiated the connection — browser processes suggest phishing or drive-by; non-browser processes suggest malware beaconing
Audit mode means the connection succeeded — check DNS logs and proxy logs for the actual data exchanged
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software connecting to a host that was recently classified as malicious by Microsoft's threat intelligence
Testing environments visiting known-bad URLs intentionally

entries.title: Network Protection blocked a connection to a malicious URL or IP
entries.summary: Network Protection prevented a connection to a malicious or untrusted destination.
entries.details: Generated when Microsoft Defender Network Protection blocks a process from connecting to a URL or IP address classified as malicious, phishing, or untrusted, and is in block mode. The connection was prevented. Key fields: the process name and the blocked destination URL or IP. A Network Protection block is a high-fidelity indicator — it means a process on the host actively attempted to reach known-malicious infrastructure and was stopped. Common scenarios: malware attempting C2 communication, ransomware reaching its key server, or a phishing page redirecting to an exploit kit. The block may indicate active malware on the host even if no EID 1116 (Defender detection) has fired yet — some malware evades signature detection but still triggers URL-based blocking.

prerequisites.description: Microsoft Defender Network Protection must be enabled and set to block mode via Group Policy, Intune, or PowerShell (Set-MpPreference -EnableNetworkProtection Enabled). Requires Microsoft Defender Antivirus with real-time protection enabled.

investigationPivots: The blocked destination URL or IP is the primary pivot — query threat intelligence for attribution, infrastructure clusters, and related IOCs
Process Name is critical: a browser process suggests a user-facing phishing or drive-by scenario; a system or background process is likely active malware beaconing
A block without a corresponding EID 1116 means the file evaded signature scanning — treat as active malware until ruled out
Correlate with Sysmon EID 3 (network connection) if available to capture the full connection attempt including source port
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software connecting to a host that was recently reclassified as malicious
False positive Microsoft threat intelligence classifications for legitimate CDN or cloud infrastructure

detectionRules.title: Network Protection Block — Malicious URL or IP
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender"
| where EventID == 1126
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Process Name">' ProcessName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="Detection URL">' BlockedURL '</Data>' *
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ProcessName, BlockedURL
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Any occurrence from a non-browser process is high priority. For browser-sourced blocks, triage based on whether the user clicked a link vs. automated background request.

detectionRules.title: Network Protection Connection Blocked
detectionRules.rule:
title: Network Protection Connection Blocked
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: windefend
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 1126
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate software connecting to recently reclassified hosts
level: high

entries.title: Controlled Folder Access blocked a sector write (ransomware MBR protection)
entries.summary: Controlled Folder Access blocked a process from performing a raw sector write to a protected disk volume.
entries.details: Generated when Controlled Folder Access (CFA) blocks a process from writing directly to disk sectors — a technique used by ransomware and wipers to encrypt or destroy the Master Boot Record (MBR), partition tables, or volume boot records, making the system unbootable. This is distinct from EID 1123 (file-level write block) — EID 1127 fires on raw sector-level writes that bypass the filesystem. This event should be treated as an active ransomware or wiper attack in progress. The most well-known scenario is BootLocker/NotPetya-style ransomware overwriting the MBR to replace it with a custom bootloader that demands ransom. Immediate isolation of the affected host is warranted.

prerequisites.description: Controlled Folder Access must be enabled and set to block mode. Sector write protection is a component of CFA that specifically monitors raw disk write operations.

investigationPivots: This event is a tier-1 incident trigger — immediate host isolation is the first response action
Identify the process attempting the sector write via the event's process field and correlate with Security EID 4688 and Sysmon EID 1 for ancestry
Check whether the MBR or VBR has already been modified by booting from external media or using forensic disk imaging tools
Correlate with concurrent file encryption activity (EID 1123 across multiple directories) to assess full ransomware scope
commonFalsePositives: Disk utility software (partition managers, cloning tools) performing legitimate sector-level writes
Virtualization or backup software that writes directly to disk images

entries.title: Controlled Folder Access audited a sector write attempt
entries.summary: Controlled Folder Access detected a raw sector write to a protected volume but allowed it (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Controlled Folder Access (CFA) is in audit mode and detects a raw sector write to a protected volume. The write was permitted to proceed. This is the audit mode counterpart to EID 1127. If this event fires from an unexpected process, the MBR or volume boot record may already have been modified. Audit mode sector write events from unknown processes warrant immediate forensic investigation of disk structures. Check the MBR integrity immediately if this event fires from a non-whitelisted disk utility.

prerequisites.description: Controlled Folder Access must be enabled and configured in audit mode.

investigationPivots: Audit mode means the sector write succeeded — verify MBR/VBR integrity immediately using bootrec or offline forensic tooling
Same investigation steps as EID 1127 — identify the writing process and assess whether it is legitimate disk tooling
Multiple EID 1128 events targeting the same volume from an unknown process confirm an active wiper or ransomware attack
commonFalsePositives: Same as EID 1127 — disk utilities and backup tools performing legitimate sector writes

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule — user overrode a warn-mode block
entries.summary: A user explicitly allowed an operation that an ASR rule flagged in warn mode.
entries.details: Generated when an ASR rule is configured in warn mode and the user chooses to dismiss the warning and allow the blocked operation to proceed. Warn mode presents a notification to the user explaining that the operation was flagged, and gives them the option to allow it. This event records that the user made that choice. Key fields: the ASR rule GUID (identifies which rule warned), the process name, and the path. From a security operations perspective, user override events are significant — they reveal which users are bypassing ASR controls, for which operations, and how often. Repeated overrides by the same user for the same rule may indicate social engineering (an attacker coached a user to click through a warning), or may indicate the rule is generating high false positives in warn mode and should be tuned.

prerequisites.description: ASR rules must be configured in warn mode (a third mode distinct from block and audit). Available from Windows 10 20H2 and Windows 11.

investigationPivots: Identify the user who clicked through the warning — repeated overrides by a single user warrant a conversation with them about what they were doing
The ASR rule GUID identifies which protection was overridden — map it to the rule name using Microsoft's ASR GUID reference
Process Name and Path reveal what was allowed — flag Office applications allowing executable creation or LSASS-access operations that the user permitted
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators legitimately dismissing ASR warnings for known-good software during deployment
End users clicking through warnings for legitimate productivity software that triggers ASR rules (common for custom macros or scripting tools)

entries.title: Scanning for viruses is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender virus scanning was disabled.
entries.details: Generated when scanning specifically for viruses is disabled. Related to but distinct from EID 5010 (malware/PUP scanning disabled) — Windows Defender distinguishes between virus scanning and the broader malware/PUP scanning category. Disabling virus scanning leaves the system without signature-based detection for executable threats. Correlate with EID 5007 to identify the configuration change, and with Security EID 4688 to identify what process initiated the change.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify the process that disabled virus scanning
Pair with EID 5010 — if both fire around the same time, most active threat detection has been disabled
Check whether any files were written or processes executed in the window between EID 5012 and scanning being re-enabled
commonFalsePositives: Managed configurations that disable specific scan types on servers to reduce I/O overhead
Coexistence configurations where a third-party AV manages virus scanning
